en: V. Somnambulic attacks in September, 1899. S. W. sits upon the sofa, leans back, shuts her eyes, breathes lightly and regularly. She gradually becomes cataleptic, the catalepsy disappears after about two minutes, when she lies in an apparently quiet sleep with complete muscular relaxation. She suddenly begins to speak in a subdued voice: "No! you take the red, I'll take the white, you can take the green, and you the blue. Are you ready? We will go now." (A pause of several minutes during which her face assumes a corpse-like pallor. Her hands feel cold and are very bloodless.) She suddenly calls out with a loud, solemn voice: "Albert, Albert, Albert," then whispering: "Now you speak," followed by a longer pause, when the pallor of the face attains the highest possible degree. Again, in a loud solemn voice, "Albert, Albert, do you not believe your father? I tell you many errors are contained in N.'s teaching. Think about it." Pause. The pallor of the face decreases. "He's very frightened. He could not speak any more." (These words in her usual conversational tone.) Pause. "He will certainly think about it," S. W. now speaks again in the same tone, in a strange idiom which sounds like French or Italian, now recalling the former, now the latter. She speaks fluently, rapidly, and with charm. It is possible to understand a few words but not to remember the whole, because the language is so strange. From time to time certain words recur, as wena , wenes , wenai , wene , etc. The absolute naturalness of the proceedings is bewildering. From time to time she pauses as if some one were answering her. Suddenly she speaks in German, "Is time already up?" (In a troubled voice.) "Must I go already? Goodbye, goodbye." With the last words there passes over her face an indescribable expression of ecstatic blessedness. She raises her arms, opens her eyes,—hitherto closed,—looks radiantly upwards. She remains a moment thus, then her arms sink slackly, her eyes shut, the expression of her face is tired and exhausted. After a short cataleptic stage she awakes with a sigh. She looks around astonished: "I've slept again, haven't I?" She is told she has been talking during the sleep, whereupon she becomes much annoyed, and this increases when she learns she has spoken in a foreign tongue. "But didn't I tell the spirits I don't want it? It mustn't be. It exhausts me too much." Begins to cry. "Oh, God! Oh, God! must then everything, everything, come back again like last time? Is nothing spared me?" The next day at the same time there was another attack. When S. W. has fallen asleep Ulrich von Gerbenstein suddenly announces himself. He is an entertaining chatterer, speaks very fluently in high German with a North-German accent. Asked what S. W. is now doing, after much circumlocution he explains that she is far away, and he is meanwhile here to look after her body, the circulation of the blood, the respiration, etc. He must take care that meanwhile no black person takes possession of her and harms her. Upon urgent questioning he relates that S. W. has gone with the others to Japan, to appear to a distant relative and to restrain him from a stupid marriage. He then announces in a whisper the exact moment when the manifestation takes place. Forbidden any conversation for a few minutes, he points to the sudden pallor occurring in S. W., remarking that materialisation at such a great distance is at the cost of correspondingly great force. He then orders cold bandages to the head to alleviate the severe headache which would occur afterwards. As the colour of the face gradually becomes more natural the conversation grows livelier. All kinds of childish jokes and trivialities are uttered; suddenly U. von G. says, "I see them coming, but they are still very far off; I see them there like a star." S. W. points to the North. We are naturally astonished, and ask why they do not come from the East, whereto U. von G. laughingly retorts: "Oh, but they come the direct way over the North Pole. I am going now; farewell." Immediately after S. W. sighs, wakes up, is ill-tempered, complains of extremely bad headache. She saw U. von G. standing by her body; what had he told us? She gets angry about the "silly chatter" from which he cannot refrain.
zh: V. 一八九九年九月的夢遊發作。S. W. 坐在沙發上，向後靠，閉上眼睛，呼吸輕柔而規律。她逐漸進入僵直狀態，約兩分鐘後僵直消失，此時她處於看似安靜的睡眠中，全身肌肉完全放鬆。她突然開始用低沉的嗓音說話：「不！你拿紅色的，我拿白色的，你可以拿綠色的，而你拿藍色的。準備好了嗎？我們現在就走。」（停頓了幾分鐘，期間她的臉色變得像屍體一樣蒼白。她的手感覺冰冷，且毫無血色。）她突然用響亮而莊嚴的聲音喊道：「阿爾伯特，阿爾伯特，阿爾伯特。」接著低語：「現在你說話。」隨後是更長的停頓，此時臉上的蒼白達到了最高程度。再次，用響亮莊嚴的聲音說：「阿爾伯特，阿爾伯特，難道你不相信你的父親嗎？我告訴你，N. 的教導中包含許多錯誤。好好想想吧。」停頓。臉上的蒼白減退了。「他非常害怕。他再也說不出話了。」（這些話用的是她平常交談的語調。）停頓。「他肯定會好好想想的。」S. W. 現在又用同樣的語調說話，但用的是聽起來像法語或義大利語的奇怪方言，時而像前者，時而像後者。她說得流暢、快速且富有魅力。可以聽懂幾個詞，但無法記住全部，因為這種語言實在太奇怪了。不時有某些詞重複出現，例如 wena、wenes、wenai、wene 等。這整個過程的絕對自然令人困惑。她不時停頓，彷彿有人在回答她。突然她用德語說：「時間已經到了嗎？」（用煩惱的聲音。）「我必須走了嗎？再見，再見。」隨著最後一句話，她的臉上掠過一種難以形容的狂喜福樂表情。她舉起雙臂，睜開眼睛——此前一直閉著——容光煥發地向上看。她保持這個姿勢片刻，然後雙臂無力地垂下，眼睛閉上，臉上的表情顯得疲倦而筋疲力竭。經過短暫的僵直階段後，她嘆了口氣醒來。她驚訝地環顧四周：「我又睡著了，對吧？」她被告知自己在睡眠中說了話，她為此非常惱火，而當她得知自己說的是外語時，惱火更甚。「但我不是告訴過那些靈體我不想要這樣嗎？這不應該發生。這太讓我疲憊了。」開始哭泣。「哦，上帝！哦，上帝！難道所有的一切，一切，都必須像上次一樣再次回來嗎？難道沒有什麼是我可以免於承受的嗎？」第二天同一時間又發生了一次發作。當 S. W. 入睡後，烏爾裡希·馮·格本斯坦突然宣告自己的到來。他是一個有趣的饒舌者，用帶有北德口音的高地德語說得非常流利。當被問及 S. W. 此刻在做什麼時，他繞了很多圈子解釋說她離得很遠，而他在此期間在這裡照顧她的身體、血液循環、呼吸等等。他必須確保在此期間沒有黑色的人佔據她的身體並傷害她。在緊迫的追問下，他透露 S. W. 和其他人一起去了日本，去給一位遠房親戚顯現，並阻止他進行一樁愚蠢的婚姻。然後他低聲宣佈了顯現發生的確切時刻。被禁止交談幾分鐘後，他指著 S. W. 身上突然出現的蒼白，評論說在如此遠的距離進行物質化需要消耗相應巨大的力量。然後他吩咐用冷敷布敷在頭上，以緩解之後會出現的劇烈頭痛。隨著臉色逐漸恢復正常，談話變得更加活躍。各種幼稚的笑話和瑣事被說出來；突然 U. von G. 說：「我看見他們來了，但他們還很遠；我看見他們在那裡，像一顆星星。」S. W. 指向北方。我們自然感到驚訝，問他們為什麼不從東方來，U. von G. 笑著反駁說：「哦，但他們是直接經過北極過來的。我現在要走了；再見。」緊接著 S. W. 嘆了口氣，醒來，情緒不好，抱怨頭痛得極其厲害。她看見 U. von G. 站在她的身體旁邊；他告訴了我們什麼？她對他那無法避免的「愚蠢閒聊」感到生氣。
